- docs-consistency-guard: Documentation agent that audits docs for staleness and inconsistency AND generates/updates documentation on request. Use proactively when reviewing any PR that touches CLAUDE.md, docs/adr/, CONTRIBUTING.md, or docs/architecture.md. Also invoke when the user asks "is the documentation up to date?", "update the milestone", "scaffold the ADR for X", "add this to the ADR index", "add a TODO item", "scaffold a README for X", or "create a doc stub for Y." It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. - migration-safety: Specialized reviewer for Alembic migrations under backend/alembic/versions/. Audits for backward-incompatible operations that would break running code during a deploy, per working-agreement (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- orchestration-abstraction-guard: Specialized reviewer that audits backend code for provider-specific branching that bypasses the OrchestrationProvider abstraction. Use this proactively when reviewing any PR that touches backend/app/orchestration/, backend/app/services/, or backend/app/api/ — especially any code path handling ADF or Airflow events, trigger bindings, or pipeline_runs. - Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. - pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. ## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

## context7
Use this server to retrieve up-to-date documentation and code examples for any library.

- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

title:	Local install: GHCR prebuilt-image path (recommended for run/self-host) + keep git-clone for contributors
state:	OPEN
author:	TheurgicDuke771 (Arijit Roy)
labels:	enhancement
comments:	1

milestone:	Week 7 — Deployment, hardening & docs
issue-type:	
parent:

blocked-by:

--
## Why

Offer **two clearly-separated local-install tracks**, matching the audiences:

- **Run / evaluate / self-host → pull the prebuilt GHCR image** (recommended). This is the BYOL on-ramp — ADR [0023](../docs/adr/0023-container-image-registry-ghcr.md) ("GHCR doubles as the post-v1 BYOL distribution registry") + ADR [0013](../docs/adr/0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md).
- **Develop / contribute → `git clone` + `scripts/setup.sh`** (unchanged — the proven, complete dev experience).

Today the docs Quickstart only documents the clone path because the image path isn't fully runnable locally yet (gaps below).

## Gaps to close (the actual work)

1. **Publish the frontend image to GHCR.** Only `dataq-backend` is pushed (`deploy.yml:30`); the frontend deploys to SWA and runs as a `node:24` dev server in compose. The `frontend/Dockerfile` + nginx exist but aren't published → a pull-and-run gives the API but **no UI**. Publish `ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-frontend` (public, anonymous pull).
2. **`docker-compose.ghcr.yml`** (or override) that **pulls** `image: ghcr.io/…:<tag>` instead of `build:`, brings up Postgres + Redis, runs the `alembic upgrade head` migrate step + demo seed, and uses **dev-bypass auth** + the **env secret store** — i.e. `docker compose -f docker-compose.ghcr.yml up` works with no source checkout.
3. **Multi-arch images** (`linux/amd64,linux/arm64` via `docker buildx`). The image is amd64-only today (no `platforms` in the build); deploy is fine (ACA is amd64) but **Apple-Silicon devs** pulling it locally run emulated/slow. Matters specifically for local-run-from-image.
4. **Stable-tag convention.** Tags are immutable `:vN` bumped per deploy (correct for deploy). Document/publish a "latest stable" pointer (a moving `:v1`/`:latest`, or doc "use the highest `:vN`") so a local-run user knows what to pull.

## Then

5. **Flip the docs Quickstart** ([docs/index.md](../docs/index.md) / [docs/getting-started.md](../docs/getting-started.md)) to **lead with the image-pull path** ("Run DataQ — prebuilt images, recommended") and keep `git clone` as the **"Develop DataQ (from source)"** path. Until 1–4 land, the Quickstart stays on `git clone` (don't point users at a half-working flow).

## Acceptance criteria

- `docker compose -f docker-compose.ghcr.yml up` (no source clone) brings up API + worker + **UI** + Postgres + Redis, runs migrations + seed, dev-bypass auth — reachable at the documented ports.
- Backend + frontend images are public on GHCR and **multi-arch** (amd64 + arm64).
- A documented stable tag to pull.
- Docs Quickstart leads with the image path; contributor path preserved.

## Sequencing

Take up **after** the other open Week-7 issues + the live testing/smoke. Tracked on Week 7; may slip to early-post-v1 BYOL if v1 ships first.
============================================

## What I implemented

The `InteractionRequiredAuthError` fallback in the axios request interceptor — [client.ts:43-58](frontend/src/api/client.ts#L43-L58):

- `acquireTokenSilent` is now wrapped in try/catch.
- On `InteractionRequiredAuthError` (expired session / revoked consent / fresh MFA), it falls back to `instance.acquireTokenRedirect(...)` — interactive auth, **consistent with `AuthGate`'s existing `loginRedirect`** (this app is redirect-flow, not popup).
- The redirect navigates the browser away, so the in-flight request is rejected rather than firing tokenless; it re-issues cleanly after MSAL completes the redirect handshake.
- **Non-interaction errors** (e.g. network failure) re-throw untouched — no spurious redirect.

## Tests (5/5 pass)

Added two cases to [client.test.ts](frontend/tests/api/client.test.ts): the redirect fallback fires with the right scopes and the request rejects; and a non-interaction error re-throws without redirecting.

## Local gates — all clean
`tsc --noEmit` ✓ · `eslint` ✓ · `prettier --check` ✓ · `vitest` 5/5 ✓

Changes are on branch `fix/issue-168-silent-token-refresh-fallback` (uncommitted).

## The one caveat — the issue's other half
The issue's "Done when" also says *"verified against a real AAD tenant."* That smoke can't be done from here — it's in the same external/live-testing bucket as the rest of Week 7. The **code** is complete and unit-tested; the real-tenant verification stays pending.

## Next step
Per your working agreements, I'd **spawn the `/code-review` agents** before committing, then commit with `Fixes #168` and open a PR. Want me to kick off the review now?

content.text:
`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.
I'll gather the diff and run the review angles.

content.text:
I reviewed the full diff directly — it's a ~15-line, self-contained change, so I traced it by hand across all the angles rather than fanning out agents for it.

## Review result: no findings

I checked the change against each angle and nothing survives as a real defect:

**Correctness**
- `instanceof InteractionRequiredAuthError` — the imported class is the same one MSAL throws in production, and the test imports it from the same module, so the type guard holds in both. ✓
- `acquireTokenRedirect` resolves/navigates, then `throw err` rejects the abandoned request — correct; the request must not proceed tokenless. ✓
- Non-interaction errors (network, etc.) fall straight through to `throw err` with no redirect — verified by the second test. ✓
- **Concurrent-request edge I specifically probed:** if several API calls hit `InteractionRequiredAuthError` at once, each calls `acquireTokenRedirect`; MSAL's `interaction_in_progress` flag makes the later ones reject with a `BrowserAuthError`. Net effect is benign — the request was being abandoned anyway and the page still redirects exactly once. Not a user-facing bug.

**Removed-behavior** — the only deleted lines are the old happy-path `acquireTokenSilent`, fully re-established inside the `try`. No dropped guard. ✓

**Cross-file** — `attachBearerToken` is a private interceptor handler; the function's contract (returns config or rejects) is unchanged for callers. ✓

**Cleanup / simplification / efficiency** — hoisting `scopes` removes the prior duplication; no redundant work, no new state. ✓

**Conventions (CLAUDE.md)** — frontend change; matches the redirect-flow pattern already used in `AuthGate` (`loginRedirect`). The "verify against a real AAD tenant" clause from #168 is a manual smoke step, out of code scope. No rule violated.

Clean. Want me to commit with `Fixes #168` and open the PR?

content.text:
Done. **PR #499 squash-merged into `main`**, branch deleted, local back on `main`, and **issue #168 auto-closed** by the merge.

## Summary

**#168 — silent token refresh `InteractionRequiredAuthError` fallback** is shipped:
- The axios request interceptor now wraps `acquireTokenSilent` and, on `InteractionRequiredAuthError`, hands off to `acquireTokenRedirect` (consistent with `AuthGate`'s `loginRedirect`), rejecting the in-flight request so it doesn't fire tokenless. Non-interaction errors re-throw untouched.
- 2 new tests; all 14 CI checks green; code-review pass found nothing.
- One CI hiccup (the msal constructor arity) caught and fixed via a `fixup:` commit before merge — root cause was my first local `tsc` running off a stale 5.14.0 install/incremental cache instead of the real `pnpm typecheck`.

## Two things worth flagging

1. **The lockfile/package.json drift is real.** `package.json` pins `@azure/msal-browser` at `5.15.0`, but `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` resolved `5.14.0` locally — and CI typechecked against a stricter msal-common than my local tree. That's a latent inconsistency that could bite other frontend work. Worth a quick `pnpm install` + lockfile reconcile (or a dedicated issue) — want me to look?

2. **The manual smoke remains open.** #168's "verified against a real AAD tenant" clause is unverified — it belongs to the pending Week-7 live-testing bucket. The code is complete and unit-tested; only the real-tenant redirect round-trip is outstanding.

That leaves **#472** (GHCR prebuilt-image local install) as the last non-epic Week-7 issue — and it explicitly defers itself behind the live testing. Want me to open an issue for the lockfile drift, or move on?

########## .github/workflows/deploy.yml ##########
name: Deploy

# Manual-only for now (workflow_dispatch): apply-ready scaffolding that never
# fires on its own until the Azure resources exist + secrets/vars are set. Once
# the infra is provisioned (see deploy/README.md), flip on the push trigger:
#   push:
#     branches: [main]
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      image_tag:
        description: "Image tag to build + deploy (blank → the commit SHA, an immutable tag)"
        default: ""

concurrency:
  group: deploy-production
  cancel-in-progress: false

# OIDC federated login to Azure (no stored client secret). The federated cred +
# the listed vars/secrets must be configured first — see deploy/README.md.
# packages:write lets the GITHUB_TOKEN push the backend image to GHCR (ADR 0023).
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  # GHCR registry (ADR 0023) — public package, lowercase owner. ACA pulls it
  # anonymously, so no registry credential is stored on the apps/job.
  #
  # Default the tag to the commit SHA (#382): `latest` is mutable, so a push
  # racing the deploy could make the migrate job, API, and worker each resolve a
  # *different* image; the SHA tag is immutable, so all three roll the exact
  # build this run produced. A manual `image_tag` input still overrides it. On a
  # push:[main] trigger `inputs` is empty, so it falls back to the SHA too.
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${{ inputs.image_tag || github.sha }}

jobs:
  backend:
    name: Backend — build, migrate, deploy (API + worker)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      - name: Azure login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v3
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # Push to GHCR (ADR 0023). The GITHUB_TOKEN has packages:write on this repo's
      # registry; no Azure/ACR credential is involved in the image push.
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build + push backend image to GHCR
        run: |
          docker build -f backend/Dockerfile -t "$IMAGE" .
          docker push "$IMAGE"

      # Migrate BEFORE rolling the app: migrations are additive/backward-compatible
      # (CLAUDE.md), so the running old code tolerates the new schema. Runs the
      # one-off migrate Container Apps Job (provisioned per deploy/README.md).
      # `az containerapp job start` only *starts* the execution and returns, so we
      # capture the execution name and poll it to a terminal state — the rollout
      # below must not begin until the migration has actually SUCCEEDED, and a
      # failed migration must abort the deploy (fail-closed) rather than roll the
      # apps onto an un-migrated schema.
      - name: Run DB migrations (alembic upgrade head) and wait for success
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Roll the job's image on the template FIRST, then start it with no
          # --image override. Passing --image to `job start` replaces the
          # container template without the Terraform-configured command
          # (`sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"`), so the execution
          # falls back to the image's default CMD (uvicorn) and never migrates.
          # Updating the template preserves that command (mirrors the api/worker
          # `az containerapp update --image` steps below).
          az containerapp job update \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"
          exec_name=$(az containerapp job start \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --query name -o tsv)
          echo "Started migration execution: $exec_name"
          # Poll to a terminal state. Besides the 15-min wall-clock cap, bound the
          # number of *consecutive* empty/unexpected statuses: a control-plane
          # hiccup or a CLI status-field rename would otherwise leave the loop
          # polling a status it never recognises until the full deadline, tying up
          # the prod concurrency group with no signal (#397). A recognised
          # in-progress status resets the counter; `|| true` keeps a transient
          # query failure a retry (counted as unknown) rather than a hard step
          # abort under `set -e`.
          deadline=$(( SECONDS + 900 ))  # 15-minute cap
          unknown=0
          unknown_max=18  # ~3 min of consecutive empty/unexpected status → abort
          while (( SECONDS < deadline )); do
            status=$(az containerapp job execution list \
              --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
              --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
              --query "[?name=='$exec_name'].properties.status | [0]" -o tsv 2>/dev/null || true)
            echo "migration status: ${status:-<pending>} (unknown streak: $unknown)"
            case "$status" in
              Succeeded) echo "Migration succeeded."; exit 0 ;;
              Failed|Degraded|Stopped) echo "::error::Migration $status — aborting before app rollout."; exit 1 ;;
              Running|Processing|Pending) unknown=0 ;;
              *)
                unknown=$(( unknown + 1 ))
                if (( unknown >= unknown_max )); then
                  echo "::error::Migration status empty/unexpected ('${status:-<empty>}') $unknown times in a row — aborting before app rollout."
                  exit 1
                fi
                ;;
            esac
            sleep 10
          done
          echo "::error::Timed out waiting for migration to complete."; exit 1

      - name: Deploy API
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.API_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

      - name: Deploy worker
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.WORKER_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

  frontend:
    name: Frontend — build + deploy to Static Web App
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    # Gate on the backend (build → migrate → roll API/worker): a failed/aborted
    # migration must stop the UI deploy, else a new SPA rolls against an un-rolled
    # or half-migrated API — a partial deploy (#382).
    needs: backend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7
      # version is required: action-setup runs at the repo root, whose
      # package.json has no `packageManager` field (it lives in frontend/).
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9.15.4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml

      - name: Build
        working-directory: frontend
        # VITE_* are inlined at build time. Non-secret azure client/tenant ids
        # come from repo vars; never the SPA secret (there is none — MSAL is public).
        env:
          VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE }}
          VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: "false"
        run: |
          pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
          pnpm build

      - name: Deploy to Azure Static Web Apps
        uses: Azure/static-web-apps-deploy@v1
        with:
          azure_static_web_apps_api_token: ${{ secrets.AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN }}
          action: upload
          app_location: frontend/dist
          skip_app_build: true
########## docker-compose.yml ##########
# Two env files (split in #209 so app Settings can run extra="forbid"):
#   .env      — infra/compose only: POSTGRES_* (+ VITE_* for the frontend). This is
#               the file compose auto-substitutes ${...} from. setup.sh creates it
#               from .env.example. NOT loaded into the api/worker containers.
#   .env.app  — app config (DATABASE_URL, AZURE_*, AUTH_DEV_BYPASS, SECRET_STORE, …):
#               only keys the app's Settings recognizes. Loaded into api/worker/
#               migrate via env_file below; created from .env.app.example.
# DATABASE_URL is identical across migrate/api/worker. Postgres user/password/db
# carry NO embedded defaults — they come from .env or the shell. The `:?` on the
# canonical postgres block below makes compose fail fast if any is unset, rather
# than baking credentials into the file.
x-database-url: &database-url "REDACTED"

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: "${POSTGRES_USER:?set POSTGRES_USER in .env}"
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: "${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:?set POSTGRES_PASSWORD in .env}"
      POSTGRES_DB: "${POSTGRES_DB:?set POSTGRES_DB in .env}"
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER}"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  # One-shot: applies Alembic migrations then exits 0. api/worker wait on it via
  # service_completed_successfully so `docker compose up` never serves an
  # un-migrated schema (#156). Mirrors setup.sh's `cd backend && alembic ...`
  # because alembic.ini's script_location is relative to backend/.
  migrate:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: "no"
    command: sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
    volumes:
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

  # FastAPI + Celery share one image (backend/Dockerfile, conda env `dataq`).
  # DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL are overridden here to the in-network service
  # hostnames (the .env defaults point at localhost for host-side dev).
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: uvicorn backend.app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # Shared across api + worker so a credential the API writes at runtime is
      # readable by connection-driven worker runs (#86). Dev only — plaintext in
      # Redis. NOTE: in redis mode KV_SECRET_* env vars are NOT consulted (that's
      # `env` mode); set SECRET_STORE=env if you rely on env-provided creds.
      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
    volumes:
      # Bind-mount source for hot reload; the conda env in the image is untouched.
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  worker:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: unless-stopped
    # -B runs celery-beat embedded in the worker (the #171 orchestration polling
    # schedule). Fine for the single-worker dev stack; prod runs a separate beat.
    command: celery -A backend.app.worker.celery_app worker -B --loglevel=INFO
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # Must match the api service so secrets the API writes are visible here (#86).
      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
    volumes:
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  # React/Vite dev server. corepack pins pnpm from package.json's packageManager.
  # node_modules is an anonymous volume so the host's (possibly macOS-built) copy
  # doesn't clobber the container's. pnpm's ~300MB content-addressable store is
  # relocated to /pnpm-store (OUTSIDE the ./frontend bind) and held in its own
  # volume — so nothing leaks into, or creates an empty mountpoint under, the host
  # working tree. The browser loads from localhost:3000 and the Vite proxy forwards
  # /api + /mcp to the api service over the compose network.
  frontend:
    image: node:24-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    working_dir: /app
    command: sh -c "corepack enable && pnpm config set store-dir /pnpm-store && pnpm install && pnpm dev --host"
    environment:
      VITE_API_PROXY_TARGET: http://api:8000
      # Pass the client-side auth vars into the Vite dev server. Only VITE_*
      # vars are forwarded (DB/secret env stays out of the node container).
      # Substituted from the project .env; dev-bypass defaults on for local dev.
      VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: ${VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS:-true}
      VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE:-user_impersonation}
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    volumes:
      - ./frontend:/app
      - /app/node_modules
      - pnpm-store:/pnpm-store
    depends_on:
      - api

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  pnpm-store:

  # *different* image; the SHA tag is immutable, so all three roll the exact
  # build this run produced. A manual `image_tag` input still overrides it. On a
  # push:[main] trigger `inputs` is empty, so it falls back to the SHA too.
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${{ inputs.image_tag || github.sha }}

jobs:
  backend:
    name: Backend — build, migrate, deploy (API + worker)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      - name: Azure login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v3
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      # Push to GHCR (ADR 0023). The GITHUB_TOKEN has packages:write on this repo's
      # registry; no Azure/ACR credential is involved in the image push.
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build + push backend image to GHCR
        run: |
          docker build -f backend/Dockerfile -t "$IMAGE" .
          docker push "$IMAGE"

      # Migrate BEFORE rolling the app: migrations are additive/backward-compatible
      # (CLAUDE.md), so the running old code tolerates the new schema. Runs the
      # one-off migrate Container Apps Job (provisioned per deploy/README.md).
      # `az containerapp job start` only *starts* the execution and returns, so we
      # capture the execution name and poll it to a terminal state — the rollout
      # below must not begin until the migration has actually SUCCEEDED, and a
      # failed migration must abort the deploy (fail-closed) rather than roll the
      # apps onto an un-migrated schema.
      - name: Run DB migrations (alembic upgrade head) and wait for success
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Roll the job's image on the template FIRST, then start it with no
          # --image override. Passing --image to `job start` replaces the
          # container template without the Terraform-configured command
          # (`sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"`), so the execution
          # falls back to the image's default CMD (uvicorn) and never migrates.
          # Updating the template preserves that command (mirrors the api/worker
          # `az containerapp update --image` steps below).
          az containerapp job update \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"
          exec_name=$(az containerapp job start \
            --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --query name -o tsv)
          echo "Started migration execution: $exec_name"
          # Poll to a terminal state. Besides the 15-min wall-clock cap, bound the
          # number of *consecutive* empty/unexpected statuses: a control-plane
          # hiccup or a CLI status-field rename would otherwise leave the loop
          # polling a status it never recognises until the full deadline, tying up
          # the prod concurrency group with no signal (#397). A recognised
          # in-progress status resets the counter; `|| true` keeps a transient
          # query failure a retry (counted as unknown) rather than a hard step
          # abort under `set -e`.
          deadline=$(( SECONDS + 900 ))  # 15-minute cap
          unknown=0
          unknown_max=18  # ~3 min of consecutive empty/unexpected status → abort
          while (( SECONDS < deadline )); do
            status=$(az containerapp job execution list \
              --name "${{ vars.MIGRATE_JOB_NAME }}" \
              --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
              --query "[?name=='$exec_name'].properties.status | [0]" -o tsv 2>/dev/null || true)
            echo "migration status: ${status:-<pending>} (unknown streak: $unknown)"
            case "$status" in
              Succeeded) echo "Migration succeeded."; exit 0 ;;
              Failed|Degraded|Stopped) echo "::error::Migration $status — aborting before app rollout."; exit 1 ;;
              Running|Processing|Pending) unknown=0 ;;
              *)
                unknown=$(( unknown + 1 ))
                if (( unknown >= unknown_max )); then
                  echo "::error::Migration status empty/unexpected ('${status:-<empty>}') $unknown times in a row — aborting before app rollout."
                  exit 1
                fi
                ;;
            esac
            sleep 10
          done
          echo "::error::Timed out waiting for migration to complete."; exit 1

      - name: Deploy API
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.API_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

      - name: Deploy worker
        run: |
          az containerapp update \
            --name "${{ vars.WORKER_APP_NAME }}" \
            --resource-group "${{ vars.AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP }}" \
            --image "$IMAGE"

  frontend:
    name: Frontend — build + deploy to Static Web App
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    # Gate on the backend (build → migrate → roll API/worker): a failed/aborted
    # migration must stop the UI deploy, else a new SPA rolls against an un-rolled
    # or half-migrated API — a partial deploy (#382).
    needs: backend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7
      # version is required: action-setup runs at the repo root, whose
      # package.json has no `packageManager` field (it lives in frontend/).
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v6
        with:
          version: 9.15.4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: "22"
          cache: pnpm
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml

      - name: Build
        working-directory: frontend
        # VITE_* are inlined at build time. Non-secret azure client/tenant ids
        # come from repo vars; never the SPA secret (there is none — MSAL is public).
        env:
          VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID }}
          VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${{ vars.VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE }}
          VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: "false"
        run: |
          pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
          pnpm build

      - name: Deploy to Azure Static Web Apps
        uses: Azure/static-web-apps-deploy@v1
        with:
          azure_static_web_apps_api_token: ${{ secrets.AZURE_STATIC_WEB_APPS_API_TOKEN }}
          action: upload
          app_location: frontend/dist
          skip_app_build: true
########## docker-compose.yml ##########
# Two env files (split in #209 so app Settings can run extra="forbid"):
#   .env      — infra/compose only: POSTGRES_* (+ VITE_* for the frontend). This is
#               the file compose auto-substitutes ${...} from. setup.sh creates it
#               from .env.example. NOT loaded into the api/worker containers.
#   .env.app  — app config (DATABASE_URL, AZURE_*, AUTH_DEV_BYPASS, SECRET_STORE, …):
#               only keys the app's Settings recognizes. Loaded into api/worker/
#               migrate via env_file below; created from .env.app.example.
# DATABASE_URL is identical across migrate/api/worker. Postgres user/password/db
# carry NO embedded defaults — they come from .env or the shell. The `:?` on the
# canonical postgres block below makes compose fail fast if any is unset, rather
# than baking credentials into the file.
x-database-url: &database-url "REDACTED"

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: "${POSTGRES_USER:?set POSTGRES_USER in .env}"
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: "${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:?set POSTGRES_PASSWORD in .env}"
      POSTGRES_DB: "${POSTGRES_DB:?set POSTGRES_DB in .env}"
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER}"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10

  # One-shot: applies Alembic migrations then exits 0. api/worker wait on it via
  # service_completed_successfully so `docker compose up` never serves an
  # un-migrated schema (#156). Mirrors setup.sh's `cd backend && alembic ...`
  # because alembic.ini's script_location is relative to backend/.
  migrate:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: "no"
    command: sh -c "cd backend && alembic upgrade head"
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
    volumes:
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

  # FastAPI + Celery share one image (backend/Dockerfile, conda env `dataq`).
  # DATABASE_URL / REDIS_URL are overridden here to the in-network service
  # hostnames (the .env defaults point at localhost for host-side dev).
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: uvicorn backend.app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # Shared across api + worker so a credential the API writes at runtime is
      # readable by connection-driven worker runs (#86). Dev only — plaintext in
      # Redis. NOTE: in redis mode KV_SECRET_* env vars are NOT consulted (that's
      # `env` mode); set SECRET_STORE=env if you rely on env-provided creds.
      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
    volumes:
      # Bind-mount source for hot reload; the conda env in the image is untouched.
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  worker:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: unless-stopped
    # -B runs celery-beat embedded in the worker (the #171 orchestration polling
    # schedule). Fine for the single-worker dev stack; prod runs a separate beat.
    command: celery -A backend.app.worker.celery_app worker -B --loglevel=INFO
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # Must match the api service so secrets the API writes are visible here (#86).
      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
    volumes:
      - ./backend:/workspace/backend
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  # React/Vite dev server. corepack pins pnpm from package.json's packageManager.
  # node_modules is an anonymous volume so the host's (possibly macOS-built) copy
  # doesn't clobber the container's. pnpm's ~300MB content-addressable store is
  # relocated to /pnpm-store (OUTSIDE the ./frontend bind) and held in its own
  # volume — so nothing leaks into, or creates an empty mountpoint under, the host
  # working tree. The browser loads from localhost:3000 and the Vite proxy forwards
  # /api + /mcp to the api service over the compose network.
  frontend:
    image: node:24-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    working_dir: /app
    command: sh -c "corepack enable && pnpm config set store-dir /pnpm-store && pnpm install && pnpm dev --host"
    environment:
      VITE_API_PROXY_TARGET: http://api:8000
      # Pass the client-side auth vars into the Vite dev server. Only VITE_*
      # vars are forwarded (DB/secret env stays out of the node container).
      # Substituted from the project .env; dev-bypass defaults on for local dev.
      VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS: ${VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS:-true}
      VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID: ${VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID:-}
      VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE: ${VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE:-user_impersonation}
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    volumes:
      - ./frontend:/app
      - /app/node_modules
      - pnpm-store:/pnpm-store
    depends_on:
      - api

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  pnpm-store:

content.content:
########## backend/Dockerfile ##########
# DataQ backend image — serves both the FastAPI API and the Celery worker.
# Build context is the repo ROOT (not backend/) because the app imports as the
# top-level `backend.app.*` package:
#   docker build -f backend/Dockerfile .
#
# Multi-stage pip build on python:3.13-slim (ADR 0025). The production image uses
# pip, NOT conda: every dependency already came through pip (environment.yml only
# `-r`'d the requirements chain — conda installed no conda-channel packages), so
# the miniconda base added ~1.5-2GB and build friction for no benefit. Conda stays
# the LOCAL-DEV tool (environment.yml / setup.sh) per CLAUDE.md; only this image
# changed. RUNTIME deps only (requirements.txt) — tests/tooling run in the dev env
# (conda locally, pip in CI), never in this image.

# ── Builder: install deps into an isolated venv ──────────────────────────────
FROM python:3.13-slim AS builder

ENV PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1

# build-essential covers any sdist-only transitive dep; it lives ONLY in this
# stage and never reaches the final image.
RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN python -m venv /opt/venv
ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH

# Copy just the requirements chain first so this layer caches on pin changes
# only, not on every code edit. requirements.txt is the runtime SINGLE SOURCE OF
# TRUTH (the dev/typecheck/tooling files are not installed into the image).
COPY backend/requirements*.txt ./backend/
RUN pip install -r backend/requirements.txt

# ── Runtime: slim base + the venv + app code ─────────────────────────────────
FROM python:3.13-slim AS runtime

# Link the GHCR package to this repo (shows under the repo's Packages and lets the
# repo's Actions GITHUB_TOKEN push to it — ADR 0023/0024). Without this the package
# is user-scoped + unlinked, so CI can't push and ACA-pull access isn't inherited.
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="DataQ backend (FastAPI API + Celery worker)"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

ENV PATH=/opt/venv/bin:$PATH \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1

WORKDIR /workspace

# The fully-built venv from the builder (no compilers / apt lists in this layer).
COPY --from=builder /opt/venv /opt/venv

# Application code + tooling config (pyproject is imported for runtime config).
COPY pyproject.toml ./
COPY backend ./backend

EXPOSE 8000

# Default to the API; the worker overrides `command` (compose / Container App),
# and the migrate job runs `alembic upgrade head`. psycopg2-binary (a wheel)
# keeps the image apt-free; the psycopg2-source swap remains a deferred nicety.
CMD ["uvicorn", "backend.app.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
########## frontend/Dockerfile ##########
# DataQ frontend image — build the Vite SPA, serve the static dist via nginx.
# Build context = frontend/ :  docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile frontend/
#
# NOTE: Azure Static Web App serves dist/ directly and does NOT need this image
# (the deploy workflow builds + uploads dist to SWA). This Dockerfile is the
# container option — e.g. running the UI on Container Apps behind the same
# ingress as the API — and keeps the UI deployable without Azure-specific glue.
#
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 8080
########## frontend nginx conf (if any) ##########
frontend/nginx.conf
frontend/nginx.conf
frontend/nginx.conf

########## frontend/nginx.conf ##########
# Serve the built SPA. SPA fallback: any non-file path returns index.html so
# client-side routes (deep links like /results/:runId) resolve to the app, while
# real assets are served directly. Listens on 8080 (non-root-friendly, the
# Container Apps default target port).
server {
    listen 8080;
    server_name _;
    root /usr/share/nginx/html;
    index index.html;

    # Long-cache fingerprinted assets; never cache index.html (it points at the
    # current asset hashes).
    location /assets/ {
        try_files $uri =404;
        expires 1y;
        add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
    }

    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;
        add_header Cache-Control "no-cache";
    }
}

########## docs/index.md ##########
# DataQ

**DataQ is a data-quality monitoring platform.** It runs automated checks against your
data — in Snowflake, ADLS Gen2, AWS S3, and Databricks Unity Catalog — tells you when
something is wrong (failed checks, stale tables, unexpected row counts), and alerts your
team. It watches your Azure Data Factory and Airflow pipelines and can run checks
automatically when a pipeline finishes.

## Who it's for

- **Data engineers / SREs** — author checks, wire up pipelines, triage failures.
- **QA / analysts** — see what passed or failed and why.
- **Product & stakeholders** — a health score and trend at a glance.

## Quickstart (5 minutes, local)

```bash
git clone https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ.git
cd DataQ
./scripts/setup.sh        # conda env + pre-commit + docker-compose + migrations
conda activate dataq
docker-compose up         # Postgres + Redis + FastAPI + React + Celery worker
```

- Backend API: `http://localhost:8000` (interactive docs at `/docs` in dev).
- Frontend: `http://localhost:3000`.

Then open the UI, add a connection, create a suite of checks, and run it. See
**[Getting started](getting-started.md)** for the full local-dev walkthrough and
**[Datasources & checks](datasources-checks.md)** to author your first check.

## Where to go next

########## frontend/nginx.conf ##########
# Serve the built SPA. SPA fallback: any non-file path returns index.html so
# client-side routes (deep links like /results/:runId) resolve to the app, while
# real assets are served directly. Listens on 8080 (non-root-friendly, the
# Container Apps default target port).
server {
    listen 8080;
    server_name _;
    root /usr/share/nginx/html;
    index index.html;

    # Long-cache fingerprinted assets; never cache index.html (it points at the
    # current asset hashes).
    location /assets/ {
        try_files $uri =404;
        expires 1y;
        add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
    }

    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;
        add_header Cache-Control "no-cache";
    }
}

# DataQ

**DataQ is a data-quality monitoring platform.** It runs automated checks against your
data — in Snowflake, ADLS Gen2, AWS S3, and Databricks Unity Catalog — tells you when
something is wrong (failed checks, stale tables, unexpected row counts), and alerts your
team. It watches your Azure Data Factory and Airflow pipelines and can run checks
automatically when a pipeline finishes.

## Who it's for

- **Data engineers / SREs** — author checks, wire up pipelines, triage failures.
- **QA / analysts** — see what passed or failed and why.
- **Product & stakeholders** — a health score and trend at a glance.

## Quickstart (5 minutes, local)

```bash
git clone https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ.git
cd DataQ
./scripts/setup.sh        # conda env + pre-commit + docker-compose + migrations
conda activate dataq
docker-compose up         # Postgres + Redis + FastAPI + React + Celery worker
```

- Backend API: `http://localhost:8000` (interactive docs at `/docs` in dev).
- Frontend: `http://localhost:3000`.

Then open the UI, add a connection, create a suite of checks, and run it. See
**[Getting started](getting-started.md)** for the full local-dev walkthrough and
**[Datasources & checks](datasources-checks.md)** to author your first check.

# Getting started (local dev)

## Prerequisites

- **conda** (the backend uses a conda env — not venv/poetry), **Docker** + Docker
  Compose, and **Node 24+ / pnpm 9+** for the frontend.

## One-command setup

```bash
git clone https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ.git
cd DataQ
./scripts/setup.sh     # creates the `dataq` conda env, installs pre-commit, pulls
                       # images, runs DB migrations, seeds dev data, writes a local .env
conda activate dataq
docker-compose up      # Postgres + Redis + FastAPI (:8000) + React (:3000) + Celery
```

Local auth uses a **dev-bypass** (no Azure tenant needed) — every request resolves to a
fixed dev user. Real Azure AD SSO is configured via environment variables in deployed
environments.

## Configuration

All runtime config is environment variables read by the backend's `Settings`. The
**complete, commented reference** is
[`.env.app.example`](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/blob/main/.env.app.example) —
copy it to `.env.app` (gitignored) and adjust. Never commit secrets; `scripts/setup.sh`
generates local-dev credentials on first run.

## Running tests

```bash
# Backend (needs a Postgres; CI provides one — locally set TEST_DATABASE_URL):
conda run -n dataq python -m pytest backend/tests

# Frontend:
cd frontend && pnpm test
```

Before pushing, run the same gate CI does: Ruff, Black `--check`, mypy, Bandit, pytest
(backend) and ESLint, Prettier `--check`, Vitest (frontend). See the
**[Contributing guide](contributing.md)**.

content.text:
I've now mapped the full picture. Before writing any infra code, here's what I found and the forks that need your call — because a couple of them shape the architecture and several pieces can't be verified locally without pushing to GHCR.

## Current state vs. the 5 gaps

| Gap | Today | Work |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Frontend image on GHCR | Only `dataq-backend` is pushed ([deploy.yml](.github/workflows/deploy.yml) build step); frontend goes to SWA. `frontend/Dockerfile` + `nginx.conf` exist but unpublished. | Build + push `dataq-frontend`. |
| 2. `docker-compose.ghcr.yml` | Only the `build:`-based dev compose exists. | New pull-based compose: PG + Redis + migrate + seed + dev-bypass + env secret store. |
| 3. Multi-arch | Both builds are amd64-only (no `buildx`/`platforms`). | `linux/amd64,linux/arm64` via buildx. |
| 4. Stable tag | Immutable `:vN` per deploy only. | A moving `:latest`/`:v1` pointer. |
| 5. Docs flip | Quickstart leads with `git clone`. | Lead with image-pull; keep clone for contributors. Gated on 1–4. |

## Two design problems the issue doesn't fully resolve

**A. The nginx image doesn't proxy `/api`.** The Vite *dev server* proxies `/api`+`/mcp` to the api container, but the published nginx image ([nginx.conf](frontend/nginx.conf)) only does SPA fallback — it has no API proxy. In the Azure deploy, SWA "links" the API as same-origin `/api`. For a no-clone `docker compose ... up` there's no SWA, so a pulled UI image would render but every API call 404s. And `VITE_*` are build-time baked, so I can't just point the SPA at `:8000` at runtime.

**B. Image publishing is conceptually separate from the Azure deploy.** `deploy.yml` is manual-dispatch, Azure-coupled, SHA-tagged (correct for ACA). Multi-arch stable-tag BYOL publishing is a different concern.

Let me get your calls on these:

questions.question: How should the GHCR images be built + published (multi-arch, stable tag, both backend + frontend)?

options.label: New publish-images.yml workflow (Recommended)
options.description: Dedicated workflow: buildx multi-arch (amd64+arm64) for both images, pushes :latest + :v1 + SHA on push-to-main/release. Leaves the working Azure deploy.yml untouched (keeps its SHA-tagged amd64 backend build for ACA).

options.label: Extend the existing deploy.yml
options.description: Add frontend build + buildx multi-arch + stable tags into the current Azure deploy workflow. Fewer files but overloads an Azure-coupled, manual-dispatch workflow with BYOL-distribution concerns.

questions.question: How should the prebuilt UI image reach the API on a no-clone `docker compose up` (same-origin)?

options.label: Env-configurable /api+/mcp proxy in nginx.conf (Recommended)
options.description: Add proxy_pass locations to nginx.conf with an env-templated upstream (default api:8000). One same-origin UI image that works in compose AND behind Container Apps ingress; no extra service.

options.label: Separate reverse-proxy service in the ghcr compose
options.description: Keep nginx.conf static; add a small proxy (e.g. nginx/caddy) service in docker-compose.ghcr.yml that fronts UI + API on one port. More moving parts, compose-only.

questions.question: How should I sequence delivery, given gaps 2 & 5 can't be fully verified until images exist on GHCR?

options.label: Staged PRs, publish-first (Recommended)
options.description: PR1: publish workflow + frontend image + multi-arch + stable tag (verify images land on GHCR). PR2: docker-compose.ghcr.yml + nginx proxy, tested against the now-real images. PR3: docs flip. One functionality per commit, each manually tested — matches the working agreements.

options.label: One PR, locally-built images
options.description: Build both images locally, tag them as the GHCR names, validate the whole compose + nginx flow locally, ship it all together, then wire the publish workflow. Faster but bundles unrelated changes.

input.content:
name: Publish images (GHCR, multi-arch)

# Builds + publishes the BYOL distribution images to GHCR — multi-arch
# (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64) so Apple-Silicon devs pull a native image, not an
# emulated one (#472). ADR 0023 (GHCR is the BYOL registry) / ADR 0013.
#
# This is DELIBERATELY separate from deploy.yml: that workflow builds an
# amd64-only, SHA-tagged BACKEND image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64, the
# immutable SHA tag is correct there). This one publishes BOTH images, multi-arch,
# under a moving `:latest` stable pointer for `docker compose ... up`-from-image
# users. The two never both fire on the same event (deploy is manual-dispatch).
#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.
          provenance: false

content.text: Good catch from the security hook — I interpolated `${{ inputs.tag }}` directly into a `run:` block, which is a workflow-injection vector (a dispatcher could set `tag` to shell metacharacters). Let me pass it via `env:` instead.

input.old_string:
- name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"

            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
input.new_string:
- name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        # Untrusted inputs come in via env (never interpolated into the script
        # body) so a dispatch `tag` value can't inject shell — IMAGE_NAME is the
        # static matrix name, INPUT_TAG the user-supplied one.
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: ${{ matrix.name }}
          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${IMAGE_NAME}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${INPUT_TAG}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi

name: Publish images (GHCR, multi-arch)

# Builds + publishes the BYOL distribution images to GHCR — multi-arch
# (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64) so Apple-Silicon devs pull a native image, not an
# emulated one (#472). ADR 0023 (GHCR is the BYOL registry) / ADR 0013.
#
# This is DELIBERATELY separate from deploy.yml: that workflow builds an
# amd64-only, SHA-tagged BACKEND image for the Azure rollout (ACA is amd64, the
# immutable SHA tag is correct there). This one publishes BOTH images, multi-arch,
# under a moving `:latest` stable pointer for `docker compose ... up`-from-image
# users. The two never both fire on the same event (deploy is manual-dispatch).
#
# Stable tag to pull = `:latest` (moving). The immutable per-deploy `:vN` backend
# tags (v7, v10, …) stay owned by deploy.yml — this workflow never writes them.
# A release-tag push (vX.Y.Z) additionally publishes that semver tag.

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Extra immutable tag to publish alongside :latest + the commit SHA (e.g. v1.0.0). Blank → just :latest + SHA."
        default: ""
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

concurrency:
  group: publish-images
  cancel-in-progress: false

# GITHUB_TOKEN needs packages:write to push to the repo-linked GHCR packages
# (ADR 0023). No Azure/registry credential is involved.
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  # Lowercase owner — GHCR paths are case-sensitive and must be lowercase.
  OWNER: theurgicduke771

jobs:
  publish:
    name: Build + push ${{ matrix.name }} (amd64 + arm64)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          # Backend build context is the repo ROOT (the app imports as the
          # top-level `backend.app.*` package — see backend/Dockerfile).
          - name: backend
            dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
            context: .
          # Frontend build context is frontend/ (its Dockerfile COPYs from there).
          - name: frontend
            dockerfile: frontend/Dockerfile
            context: frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      # QEMU provides the arm64 emulation the amd64 runner needs to build the
      # non-native platform; buildx drives the multi-platform build + manifest.
      - uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v4
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute image tags
        id: tags
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          image="${REGISTRY}/${OWNER}/dataq-${{ matrix.name }}"
          # Always: the moving stable pointer + the immutable commit SHA.
          tags="${image}:latest"$'\n'"${image}:${GITHUB_SHA}"
          # On a release-tag push, github.ref_name is the tag (vX.Y.Z); on a
          # manual run, the optional `tag` input. Publish it as a third tag.
          extra="${{ inputs.tag }}"
          if [ -z "$extra" ] && [ "${GITHUB_REF_TYPE}" = "tag" ]; then
            extra="${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
          fi
          if [ -n "$extra" ]; then
            tags="${tags}"$'\n'"${image}:${extra}"
          fi
          echo "Publishing tags:"; echo "$tags"
          # Multi-line output for the build-push-action `tags:` list.
          {
            echo "tags<<EOF"
            echo "$tags"
            echo "EOF"
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Build + push (multi-arch)
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          file: ${{ matrix.dockerfile }}
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.tags.outputs.tags }}
          # Attestations off: keep the published manifest a plain multi-arch index
          # (no extra unknown/attestation entries) so anonymous ACA/compose pulls
          # resolve cleanly.

# DataQ frontend image — build the Vite SPA, serve the static dist via nginx.
# Build context = frontend/ :  docker build -f frontend/Dockerfile frontend/
#
# NOTE: Azure Static Web App serves dist/ directly and does NOT need this image
# (the deploy workflow builds + uploads dist to SWA). This Dockerfile is the
# container option — e.g. running the UI on Container Apps behind the same
# ingress as the API — and keeps the UI deployable without Azure-specific glue.
#
# VITE_* values are inlined at build time (Vite bakes them into the bundle), so
# they're build args, not runtime env. Pass them with --build-arg.

FROM node:22-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
RUN corepack enable

COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

COPY . .
ARG VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=""
ARG VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE="user_impersonation"
ARG VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS="false"
ENV VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_SPA_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID=$VITE_AZURE_API_CLIENT_ID \
    VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE=$VITE_AZURE_API_SCOPE \
    VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=$VITE_AUTH_DEV_BYPASS
RUN pnpm build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine AS serve
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 8080
